Question: The three overriding considerations of a lender's mortgage loan decision are
A: points, interest rate, and loan term
B: the location of the mortgaged property, the borrower's cash, and the amount of the borrower's equity
C: the ability to re-pay, the value of the collateral, and the profitability of the loan
D: the amount of the loan, the borrower's income, and the down payment
Feedback / Explanation: A lender assesses risks by examining, or qualifying, both borrower and property. In qualifying a borrower, an underwriter weighs the ability of the borrower to repay the loan. In qualifying a property, an underwriter assesses the ability of the property value to cover potential losses. In this evaluation, a lender requires that the appraised value of the property be more than adequate to cover the contemplated loan and costs. Finally, the loan must make money for the lending organization as a basic business precept

Question: The loan-to-value ratio is an important underwriting criterion, for the primary reason that
A: borrowers with no equity will default and abandon the property
B: the lender wants to ensure the loan is fully collateralized
C: a borrower can only afford to borrow a portion of the entire purchase price
D: a fair amount of borrower equity demonstrates good faith
Feedback / Explanation: In qualifying a property, an underwriter assesses the ability of the property value to cover potential losses. In this evaluation, a lender requires that the appraised value of the property be more than adequate to cover the contemplated loan and costs. To protect further against loss, a lender will usually lend only a portion of the property's value. The relationship of the loan amount to the property value, expressed as a percentage, is called the loan-to-value ratio, or LTV

Question: The Equal Credit Opportunity Act (ECOA) requires lenders to
A: extend equal credit to all prospective borrowers
B: consider the income of a spouse in evaluating a family's creditworthiness
C: discount the income of a person involved in child-rearing or child-bearing
D: specialize lending activity by geographical area for improved customer service
Feedback / Explanation: The Equal Credit Opportunity Act (ECOA) requires a lender to evaluate a loan applicant on the basis of that applicant's own income and credit rating, unless the applicant requests the inclusion of another's income and credit rating in the application. In such a case, a lender may not discount or disregard income from part-time work, a spouse, child support, alimony, or separate maintenance

Question: The purpose of an income ratio in qualifying a borrower is to
B: compare one's earnings to one's short-term debt
C: identify the highest possible interest rate that the borrower can afford
D: quantify the borrower's assets to the fullest extent
Feedback / Explanation: Lenders want to be assured that the borrower has adequate means to make all necessary periodic payments on the loan in addition to other housing expenses and debts such as credit card payments and car payments. Most lenders use two ratios to estimate an applicant's ability to fulfill a loan obligation: an income ratio, or housing ratio, and a debt ratio, or housing plus debt ratio. The income ratio, or housing expense ratio, establishes borrowing capacity by limiting the percent of gross income a borrower may spend on housing costs

Question: A borrower's debt ratio is derived by
A: dividing one's total debt by one's debt payments
B: dividing one's gross income by one's assets
C: dividing one's gross income by one's debts
D: dividing one's debts by one's gross income
Feedback / Explanation: The debt ratio formula is:  (monthly housing expense + monthly debt obligations) ÷ monthly gross income = debt ratio

Question: A lender's commitment to lend funds to a borrower in order to retire another outstanding loan is called a
Feedback / Explanation: A take-out commitment offers to make a loan that will "take out" another lender's loan, i.e., pay it off and replace it. The take-out loan is most often used to retire a construction loan. The take-out lender agrees to pay off the short-term construction loan by issuing a long-term permanent loan

Question: At the closing of a mortgage loan
A: the borrower pays off the note and receives clear title
B: the lender issues a firm loan commitment
C: the parties complete all loan origination documents and the loan is funded
D: the borrower's loan application is complete and the file closed
Feedback / Explanation: Closing of a mortgage loan normally occurs with the closing of the real estate transaction. At the real estate closing, the lender typically has deposited the funded amount with an escrow agent, along with instructions for disbursing the funds. The borrower deposits necessary funds with the escrow agent, executes final documents, and receives signed copies of all relevant documents

Question: Which laws or regulations require mortgage lenders to disclose financing costs and annual percentage rate to a borrower before funding a loan?
C: The Real Estate Settlement Procedures Act
Feedback / Explanation: Truth-in-Lending Laws, implemented by the Federal Reserve's Regulation Z, regulate the disclosure of costs, the right to rescind the credit transaction, advertising credit offers, and penalties for non-compliance with the act

Question: Which laws or regulations prevent mortgage lenders from discriminating in extending credit to potential borrowers based on race, color, religion, national origin, sex, marital status, age, and dependency on public assistance?
C: The Real Estate Settlement Procedures Act
Feedback / Explanation: The Equal Credit Opportunity Act prohibits discrimination in extending credit based on race, color, religion, national origin, sex, marital status, age, or dependency upon public assistance

Question: Which laws or regulations require mortgage lenders to provide an estimate of closing costs to a borrower and forbid them to pay kickbacks for referrals?
C: the Real Estate Settlement Procedures Act
Feedback / Explanation: RESPA is a federal law which aims to standardize settlement practices and ensure that buyers understand settlement costs. RESPA applies to purchases of residential real estate (one- to four-family homes) to be financed by "federally related" first mortgage loans. In addition to imposing settlement procedures, RESPA provisions prohibit lenders from paying kickbacks and unearned fees to parties who may have helped the lender obtain the borrower's business

Question: The Federal Reserve System regulates the money supply in which of the following ways?
A: Selling securities, printing money, and controlling lending underwriting requirements
B: Buying securities, changing the discount rate, and controlling banking reserves
C: Printing money, changing interest rates, and selling T-bills
D: Controlling the prime rate, trading securities, and purchasing loans
Feedback / Explanation: The Federal Reserve System regulates the money supply by means of three methods:  •selling or re-purchasing government securities, primarily Treasury bills  •changing the reserve requirement for member banks  •changing the interest rate, or discount rate, the system charges member institutions for borrowing funds from the Federal Reserve System central banks

Question: One of the primary purposes for the secondary mortgage market is to
A: cycle funds back to primary lenders so they can make more loans
B: issue second mortgages and sell them in the home equity market
C: lend funds to banks so they can make more loans
D: pay off defaulted loans made by primary mortgage lenders
Feedback / Explanation: By purchasing loans from primary lenders, the secondary market returns funds to the primary lenders, thereby enabling the primary lender to originate more mortgage loans

Question: The major players in the secondary mortgage market are
A: Fannie Mae, Freddie Mac, and Ginnie Mae
D: Fannie Mae, Freddie Mac, and the Federal Reserve
Feedback / Explanation: As major players in the secondary market, the Federal National Mortgage Association (FNMA, "Fannie Mae"), Government National Mortgage Association (GNMA, "Ginnie Mae), and Federal Home Loan Mortgage Corporation (FHLMC, "Freddie Mac") tend to set the standards for the primary market

Question: A principal role of FNMA is to
Feedback / Explanation: Federal National Mortgage Association, or Fannie Mae, buys conventional, FHA-backed and VA-backed loans

Question: The primary role of the Federal Housing Authority in the mortgage lending market is to
A: guarantee loans made by approved lenders
B: insure loans made by approved lenders
C: purchase loans made by approved lenders
D: originate loans made by approved lenders
Feedback / Explanation: The Federal Housing Administration (FHA) does not lend money, but insures permanent long-term loans made by others. The lender must be approved by the FHA, and the borrower must meet certain FHA qualifications

Question: The principal role of the Veteran's Administration in the mortgage lending market is to
A: guarantee loans made by approved lenders
B: insure loans made by approved lenders
C: purchase loans made by approved lenders
D: originate loans made by approved lenders
Feedback / Explanation: The Veterans Administration (Department of Veterans Affairs) offers loan guarantees to qualified veterans. The VA, like the FHA, does not lend money except in certain areas where other financing is not generally available. Instead, the VA partially guarantees permanent long-term loans originated by VA-approved lenders on properties that meet VA standards

Question: A graduated payment loan is a mortgage loan where
A: loan funds are disbursed to the borrower on a graduated basis
B: the interest rate periodically increases in graduated phases
D: the loan payments gradually increase and the loan term gradually decreases
Feedback / Explanation: With a graduated payment mortgage, the payments at the beginning of the loan term are not sufficient to amortize the loan fully, and unpaid interest is added to the principal balance. Payments are later adjusted to a level that will fully amortize the loan's increased balance over the remaining loan term

Question: A buydown is a financing arrangement where
A: the lender lowers the interest rate on a loan in exchange for a prepayment of principal
B: the borrower pays additional interest at the onset in order to obtain a lower interest rate
C: the lender requires the borrower to buy down the price of the property by increasing the down payment
D: the borrower pays the lender additional funds to buy down the term of the loan
Feedback / Explanation: A buydown loan entails a prepayment of interest on a loan. The prepayment effectively lowers the interest rate and the periodic payments for the borrower

Question: The key feature of an adjustable mortgage loan is that
B: the monthly payment increases over the life of the loan
C: the principal balance does not amortize
D: the loan term can be shortened or lengthened
Feedback / Explanation: Adjustable rate mortgages (ARMs) allow the lender to change the interest rate at specified intervals and by a specified amount. Federal regulations place limits on incremental interest rate increases and on the total amount by which the rate may be increased over the loan term

Question: One feature of a wraparound mortgage loan is that
A: the loan is a senior loan
B: the seller offering the buyer a wraparound can profit from a difference in interest rates
C: the underlying loan must be retired
D: the second mortgage borrower may make payments directly to the first mortgage lender
Feedback / Explanation: In a wraparound loan arrangement, the seller receives a junior mortgage from the buyer, and uses the buyer's payments to make the payments on the original first mortgage

Question: A builder is required to secure a loan with mortgages on three properties. This is an example of
Feedback / Explanation: A blanket mortgage is secured by more than one property, such as multiple parcels of real estate in a development

Question: Which of the following is true of a loan with negative amortization?
A: The loan is an interest-only loan
B: Payments are not sufficient to retire the loan
C: The loan balance is diminishing, or going negative
D: Additional interest is being added to the monthly payment
Feedback / Explanation: Negative amortization causes the loan balance to increase over the term. This occurs if the borrower's periodic payment is insufficient to cover the interest owed for the period. The lender adds the amount of unpaid interest to the borrower's loan balance

Question: Hopeful homeowner Henry has an eye on a house valued at $600,000. His lender’s LTV is 80%. How much cash will Henry need to put in for a downpayment?
Feedback / Explanation: $600,000 times 20% (100% - the 80% for the loan) for the downpayment is $120,000.

Question: A bank is offering Henry a 30-year loan of $300,000 at a rate of 4.5%. The loan constant for this loan is 5.07. How much will Henry have to pay for monthly principal and interest?
Feedback / Explanation: 300 ($300,000 / 1,000) times the loan constant of 5.07 is $1,521 for PI each month.

Question: Mary is paying $1,800 per month in principal and interest on a 20-year loan at 4% interest but can’t remember how much she originally borrowed. The loan constant is 6.06. Can you help?
Feedback / Explanation: $1,800 per month divided by the loan constant of 6.06 is 297.03.  Multiple 297.03 by 1,000 to get the loan value of $297,030.

Question: A valid mortgage or trust deed is evidence of
A: collateral for the payment of monies due
C: transfer of title from owner to lender
D: borrower’s ability to repay the loan
Feedback / Explanation: A valid mortgage or trust deed financing arrangement requires a note as evidence of the debt

Question: The mortgagor in a mortgage financing transaction is the
C: representative of the title insurance company
D: previous owner of the subject property
Feedback / Explanation: A mortgage is a legal document stating the pledge of the borrower’s property as collateral security to the lender (the mortgagee).  The  borrower is the mortgagor.  (Remember both the words “lender” and “mortgagee” have two ‘e’s.)

Question: A deed of trust conveys title to the property
A: from the borrower to a trustee
B: from the lender to a trustee
D: for a period of one year
Feedback / Explanation: A deed of trust conveys title to the property in question from the borrower (trustor) to a trustee as security for the loan. The trustee is a third party fiduciary to the trust. While the loan is in place, the trustee holds the title on behalf of the lender, who is the beneficiary of the trust

Question: The principal amount of the loan is
A: the amount of money borrowed on which interest payments are calculated
B: the purchase price less the amount of the appraised value
C: the purchase price minus the down payment
D: the sum of the total amount borrowed plus the down payment
Feedback / Explanation: The capital amount borrowed, on which interest payments are calculated, is the original loan principal

Question: A discount point is
A: an amount deducted from loan origination fees as a thank-you for selecting that lender
B: an amount above which a discount is given to a qualified borrower
Feedback / Explanation: A discount point is one percent of the loan amount. Thus, one point on a $100,000 loan equals $1,000. The lender charges this as pre-paid interest at closing by funding only the face amount of the loan minus the discount points

Question: If a loan amount is $200,000 and the borrower is paying 2 points, the cost of those points is
A promissory note sets forth the
A: loan amount, term of loan, and interest rate
B: loan amount, term of loan, interest rate and method of repayment
C: loan amount, term of loan, interest rate, method and timing for repayment
D: loan amount, term of loan, interest rate, method and timing for repayment, and borrower’s promise to repay
Feedback / Explanation: A borrower who executes a promissory note is the maker or payer of the note. The lender is the payee. To be properly executed, all parties who have an interest in the property should sign the note. The note sets forth: the loan amount, the term of the loan, the method and timing of repayment, the interest rate to be paid, the borrower's promise to pay

Question: When applying for a loan, a borrower will need to provide documentation that might include which of the following?
A: A credit report and verification of income
C: The employment history for the past ten years
D: The home addresses for the past 12 years
Feedback / Explanation: The process of initiating a mortgage loan begins when a borrower completes a loan application and submits it to a lender for evaluation by the lender's underwriters. The lender will require supporting documents as part of the application process which will include a credit report and verification of income

Question: The financial components of a mortgage loan include
B: principal, interest & interest rate, points, term, and payments
C: term, amount of loan, and PMI
Feedback / Explanation: The financial components of a mortgage loan include the principal, interest and interest rate, term and payments

Question: Private mortgage insurance (PMI) may be required by the lender on loans that have a down payment of
Feedback / Explanation: A lender may seek further protection against risk by requiring the borrower to obtain private mortgage insurance. This is frequently the case with loans requiring a relatively small down payment, leading to a high loan-to-value ratio

Question: The process of securing a loan by pledging a property without giving up ownership of the property is called
Feedback / Explanation: The process of securing a loan by pledging a property without giving up ownership of the property is called hypothecation

Question: A state in which the laws consider the mortgage to be a lien against the property and where the lender receives equitable title rather than legal title to the property is known as
Feedback / Explanation: A state whose laws give a lender on a mortgaged property equitable title rather than legal title. The mortgagor in a lien theory state retains legal title

Question: A state in which the laws give the legal title of a mortgaged property to the lender until the borrower satisfies the terms and obligations of the loan is known as
Feedback / Explanation: A state whose laws give legal title of a mortgaged property to the mortgagee until the mortgagor satisfies the terms and obligations of the loan

Question: A borrower who executes a mortgage is known as the
Feedback / Explanation: A mortgage is a legal document stating the pledge of the borrower (the mortgagor) to the lender (the mortgagee).

Question: A borrower who executes a promissory note is known as the
Feedback / Explanation: A borrower who executes a promissory note is the maker or payer of the note. The lender is the payee

Question: In states where a trust deed is used instead of a mortgage, the borrower is referred to as the
Feedback / Explanation: In the case of a deed of trust, the lender as beneficiary requests the trustee to execute a release deed or deed of reconveyance to the borrower as trustor

Question: If a borrower pays more than the scheduled payment amount on a loan, the excess amount paid is
A: added to the escrow account for payment of taxes
B: credited to the principal balance of the loan
C: added to the amount of the loan principal
D: returned to the borrower within five business days
Feedback / Explanation: If a borrower pays more than the scheduled payment amount, the excess amount paid is credited to the principal balance of the loan

Question: If the actual title to the property is not given to the borrower until the loan is paid in full
A: the property is located in a lien theory state
B: the property is located in a title theory state
C: the borrower has not made all payments as agreed
D: the mortgage was not recorded as it should have been
Feedback / Explanation: States differ in their interpretation of who owns mortgaged property. Those that regard the mortgage as a lien held by the mortgagee (lender) against the property owned by the mortgagor (borrower) are called lien-theory states. Those that regard the mortgage document as a conveyance of ownership from the mortgagor to the mortgagee are called title-theory states

Question: Periodic payments of real estate taxes and insurance for the property are sometimes required by the lender to be held in a special account to ensure payment.  Such an account is referred to as
Feedback / Explanation: The lender often requires the buyer to establish an escrow account to cover the real estate taxes and hazard insurance

Question: Which of the following is information that a lender will seek from borrowers to determine their ability to repay the loan?
Feedback / Explanation: The lender must rely on eight types of information to determine that the borrower has the ability to repay the loan: current income or assets (excluding the value of the mortgaged property), current employment status, credit history, monthly payment for the mortgage, monthly payments being made on other loans on the same property, monthly payments for other mortgage-related expenses, other debts, monthly debt payments compared to monthly income (debt-to-income ratio)

Question: A Qualified Mortgage is one that meets the “ability to repay” requirements.  It has certain required features and is not allowed to have others.  Which of the following features is allowed when issuing such a mortgage?
B: The absence of a cap on upfront points and fees
D: Monthly debt ratio of 43% or less
Feedback / Explanation: Issuing a Qualified Mortgage gives the lender certain legal protections in case the borrower fails to repay the loan. The following are generally not allowed: an “interest-only” period--when interest, but not principal, is being repaid, negative amortization–when principal increases over time, balloon payment–larger than normal payment at the end of the loan term, loan term longer than 30 years, excessive upfront fees and points.  The following are generally required: monthly debt no more than 43 % of monthly pre-tax income, limits on points

Question: A borrower’s net worth is sometimes important to the lender since it indicates
A: the extent to which assets exceed liabilities
B: the borrower’s initiative to repay the loan
Feedback / Explanation: An applicant's net worth shows a lender the depth of the applicant's cash reserves, the value and liquidity of assets, and the extent to which assets exceed liabilities. These facts are important to a lender as an indication of the applicant's ability to sustain debt payment in the event of loss of employment

Question: Before issuing a loan commitment, the lender must qualify the potential borrower by requesting certain documentation and submitting it to the appropriate parties for review.  These parties are known as the
Feedback / Explanation: When a lender's underwriters have qualified an applicant and the lender has decided to offer the loan, the lender gives the applicant a written notice of the agreement to lend under specific terms

Question: Once the loan has been approved, the borrower will receive notice by way of a written promise called the loan
Feedback / Explanation: The lender gives the applicant a written notice of the agreement to lend under specific terms. This written promise is the loan commitment. The commitment may take a number of common forms, including a firm commitment, a lock-in commitment, a conditional commitment, and a take-out commitment

Question: Which of the following takes place prior to closing?
A: The lender deposits the funded amount with the closing or escrow agent
B: The lender authorizes the real estate broker to close the transaction
C: The borrower pre-pays all real estate taxes up to and including the time of closing
D: The real estate broker transfers the title to the mortgaged property to the buyer
Feedback / Explanation: At or before the real estate closing, the lender deposits the borrower’s loan proceeds with an escrow agent, along with instructions for disbursing the funds

Question: Under the terms of Regulation Z, which of the following statements is true?
A: The lender must disclose all finance charges as well as the APR in advance of closing
B: Willful violation of Regulation Z may be punishable by imprisonment of up to ten years
C: Financial cost disclosures do not need to be presented in writing
D: The borrower has a right to cancel within seven days of the completion of the transaction
Feedback / Explanation: Under Regulation Z, a lender must disclose all finance charges as well as the true annualized percentage rate (APR) in advance of closing. A lender does not have to show the total interest payable over the loan term or include in finance charges such settlement costs as fees for appraisal, title, credit report, survey, or legal work. Disclosure must be distinctly presented in writing

Question: The Equal Credit Opportunity Act (ECOA), in addition to nondiscrimination requirements, requires the lender to
A: report any such rejection to the credit reporting agency within 30 days
B: avoid speaking directly with the applicant after the denial has been sent
C: refund all fees paid by the borrower for the costs of the credit reporting within 10 days
D: inform a rejected applicant in writing of reasons for denial within 30 days
Feedback / Explanation: A lender must inform a rejected applicant in writing of reasons for denial within 30 days. A creditor who fails to comply is liable for punitive and actual damages

Question: The Real Estate Settlement Procedures Act (RESPA) is a federal law that is designed to
A: account for monies disbursed at the closing of the transaction
B: give direction to the closing agent regarding the disbursement of funds
C: standardize settlement practices and ensure that borrowers understand settlement costs
D: give direction to the borrowers regarding the funds needed to close
Feedback / Explanation: The Real Estate Settlement Procedures Act (RESPA) is a federal law that aims to standardize settlement practices and ensure that buyers understand settlement costs. RESPA applies to purchases of residential real estate (one- to four-family homes) to be financed by "federally related" first mortgage loans

Question: The Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) requires lenders to use which of the following two forms?
A: Lender Disclosure of Settlement Costs and Borrower’s Receipt of Documents
B: Lender Financial Statement and Borrower’s Consent to Closing
C: Estimate of Loan Proceeds and Disclosure of Closing Charges
Feedback / Explanation: The Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) requires lenders to use two specific forms to disclose settlement costs to the buyer. A lender must provide a Loan Estimate (H-24) within three days of receiving the loan application and allow the buyer to see the Closing Disclosure (H-25) three days before loan consummation. A lender must also provide a buyer with a copy of the information booklet, “Your Home Loan Toolkit,” concerning mortgage loan, closing costs and closing procedures

Question: The primary mortgage market consists of lenders who originate mortgage loans directly to
Feedback / Explanation: The primary mortgage market consists of lenders who originate mortgage loans directly to borrowers. Primary mortgage market lenders include: savings and loans, commercial banks, mutual savings banks, life insurance companies, mortgage bankers, credit unions

Question: Which of the following would NOT be a part of the secondary mortgage market?
Feedback / Explanation: Secondary mortgage market organizations include: Federal National Mortgage Association (FNMA, or Fannie Mae), Federal Home Loan Mortgage Corporation (FHLMC, or Freddie Mac), Government National Mortgage Association (GNMA, or Ginnie Mae), investment firms that assemble loans into packages and sell securities based on the pooled mortgages, life insurance companies, pension funds, primary market institutions who also invest as secondary lenders

Question: Which of the following statements describes a unique characteristic of portfolio lenders?
A: They originate loans to retain in their loan portfolios
B: They are not required to follow regulations of the primary and secondary mortgage markets
C: They may legally discriminate regarding who they will accept as borrowers
Feedback / Explanation: A primary mortgage market lender may or may not sell its loans into the secondary market. Many lenders originate loans for the purpose of retaining the investments in their own loan portfolio. These loans are referred to as portfolio loans, and lenders originating loans for their own portfolio are called portfolio lenders

Question: Which of the following organizations is part of the secondary mortgage market?
Feedback / Explanation: Secondary mortgage market organizations include: Federal National Mortgage Association (FNMA, or Fannie Mae), Federal Home Loan Mortgage Corporation (FHLMC, or Freddie Mac), Government National Mortgage Association (GNMA, or Ginnie Mae), investment firms that assemble loans into packages and sell securities based on the pooled mortgages, life insurance companies, pension funds, primary market institutions who also invest as secondary lenders

Question: Which of the following statements is true when describing a Federal Housing Administration (FHA) mortgage?
A: There are no specific regulations regarding appraisals
B: If borrower defaults, FHA reimburses the lender for losses due to default by the borrower
D: There is no maximum length of the repayment period
Feedback / Explanation: The Federal Housing Administration (FHA) does not lend money but insures permanent long-term loans made by others. The FHA ensures that the lender will not suffer significant loss in the case of borrower default. To provide this security, FHA provides insurance and charges the borrower an insurance premium

Question: Which of the following is a requirement for eligibility for a VA loan?
A: A veteran must apply for a Certificate of Eligibility
B: The borrower must be on active duty
C: A down payment of 5% is required
Feedback / Explanation: A veteran must apply for a Certificate of Eligibility to find out how much the VA will guarantee in a particular situation

Question: A loan with the same interest rate over a specified term with no rate increase is known as
Feedback / Explanation: A loan with the same interest rate over a specified term with no rate increase is known as a fixed rate loan

Question: A junior loan is defined as a loan that
A: has a loan amount less than the senior loan
B: is adjustable on an annual basis
C: is subordinate to a senior or first mortgage loan
D: was initiated subsequent to the primary mortgage
Feedback / Explanation: When there are multiple loans on a single property, there is an order of priority in the liens which the mortgages create. The first, or senior, loan generally has priority over any subsequent loans. Second loans are riskier than first loans because the senior lender will be satisfied first in case of default

Question: A negative amortization loan
A: results in the borrower owing less at the end of the loan term than with a conventional loan.
B: gives the borrower no flexibility in repaying the loan
C: results in the borrower potentially owing more at the end of the loan term
D: requires that the lender lessen the amount of the amortization on an annual basis
Feedback / Explanation: Negative amortization is a situation in which the loan balance of an amortizing loan increases because periodic payments are insufficient to pay all interest owed for the period. Unpaid interest is added to the principal balance

Question: Seller financing involves the seller providing some or all of the financing for the buyer’s purchase.  One of the most common types of seller financing is called the
Feedback / Explanation: The seller may provide some or all of the financing for the buyer's purchase. Some of the most common methods of seller financing are purchase money mortgages, including the wraparound, and the contract for deed

Question: Which of the following is known as a special purpose loan?
Feedback / Explanation: The following are special-purpose loans: home equity, package, construction, bridge, equity participation, take-out, reverse annuity, and blanket

Question: If the real estate taxes are $5,800 per year and the insurance is $200 per year, what is the amount that will be escrowed each month?
First-time homebuyers want to purchase a home for $200,000.  They have $20,000 for a down payment. What is the principal amount of the loan?
First-time homebuyers want to purchase a home for $200,000.  They have $20,000 for a down payment. What is the percentage of loan to value (LTV)?
Feedback / Explanation: $180,000 divided by $200,000=.9 which is 90%

Question: First-time homebuyers want to purchase a home for $200,000.  They have $20,000 for a down payment. Will private mortgage insurance (PMI) be required for this transaction?
A: Yes, because the down payment is less than 20%
B: No, if the borrower’s credit score has been determined to be adequate
C: No, because the borrower’s homeowner’s insurance would cover any loss
D: Yes, if the borrower’s credit score was determined to be under 700
Feedback / Explanation: Private mortgage insurance (PMI) protects the lender against loss of a portion of the loan (typically 20-25%) in case of borrower default. Private mortgage insurance generally applies to loans that are not backed by the Federal Housing Administration (FHA) or Veterans Administration (VA) and that have a down payment of less than 20% of the property value

Question: In a ____________ mortgage, a homeowner pledges the equity in the home as security for a loan which is paid out in regular monthly amounts over the term of the loan
Feedback / Explanation: In a reverse annuity mortgage, a homeowner pledges the equity in the home as security for a loan which is paid out in regular monthly amounts over the term of the loan. The homeowner, in effect, is able to convert the equity to cash without losing ownership and possession

Question: In a ____________ loan arrangement, the seller receives a junior mortgage from the buyer and uses the buyer's payments to make the payments on the original first mortgage
Feedback / Explanation: In a wraparound loan arrangement, the seller receives a junior mortgage from the buyer and uses the buyer's payments to make the payments on the original first mortgage

Question: The mortgage loan documents stipulate that which of the following conditions must be met during the term of the loan?
A: The borrower must keep the property insured as required by the lender
B: The borrower must maintain the property’s landscaping
C: The borrower must maintain the property in excellent operating condition
D: The borrower must seek the lender’s approval if there is a desire to sell the property
Feedback / Explanation: The borrower must keep the property insured as the lender requires. Insurance proceeds, in case of a claim, are applied first to restoring the property, or, if that is not feasible, to payment of the debt

Question: The minimum down payment required to obtain an FHA loan is
Feedback / Explanation: The minimum down payment for an FHA-backed loan is based on the lower of the appraised value or the sales price. The present requirement for single-family residential loans is 3.5%

Question: The minimum down payment required on a VA loan, if the borrower qualifies, is
Feedback / Explanation: The VA usually requires no down payment, although the lender may require one

Question: A Certificate of Eligibility is required to qualify for
Feedback / Explanation: A veteran must apply for a Certificate of Eligibility to find out how much the VA will guarantee in a particular situation

Question: The clause in the mortgage that could allow the lender to require the borrower to repay the loan before the scheduled date is called
Feedback / Explanation: The acceleration clause grants the lender the right to call all sums immediately due and payable on a loan should the borrower violate certain provisions of the loan agreement

Question: The underwriting process includes which of the following?
A: Appraising the value of personal property
B: Determining the loan terms based on the location of the property
C: Reviewing the borrower’s history of payment of loans or rent on previous properties
Feedback / Explanation: A lender assesses risks by examining, or qualifying both borrower and property. In qualifying a borrower, an underwriter weighs the ability of the borrower to repay the loan. This requires an analysis of whether the borrower's income, cash resources, creditworthiness, net worth, and employment stability meet the lender's standards

Question: Under the terms of the Equal Credit Opportunity Act, a lender may NOT do which of the following?
A: Ask applicants any question about their age, sex, religion, race, or national origin except as the law may require
B: Require that borrowers disclose all income, debt, cash, and net worth
C: Evaluate a loan applicant based on the applicant’s income and credit rating
D: Require that a borrower demonstrate creditworthiness
Feedback / Explanation: The ECOA has prohibited a number of practices in mortgage loan underwriting. Including asking applicants any question about their age, sex, religion, race or national origin, except as the law may require

Question: Which of the following is a ratio used by lenders to determine whether or not the borrower has adequate means to make the periodic payments?
Feedback / Explanation: The debt ratio considers all of the monthly obligations of the income ratio plus any additional monthly payments the applicant must make for other debts. The lender will look specifically at minimum monthly payments due on revolving credit debts and other consumer loans

Question: Which of the following statements is true under the general terms of a contract for deed?
A: The seller retains the title, and the buyer receives possession and equitable title while making payments
B: The buyer will receive the title to the property at the closing of the transaction
C: The buyer will receive the title to the property and possession of the property at the closing of the transaction
D: The seller will retain possession of the property until 50% of the loan amount is paid
Feedback / Explanation: Under a contract for deed arrangement, the seller retains title and the buyer receives possession and equitable title while making payments under the terms of the contract. The seller conveys title when the contract has been fully performed

Question: Julio just purchased a house for $650,000 with a $600,000 mortgage loan and $50,000 cash downpayment.  Which of the following will Julio's lender most likely require Julio purchase?
Feedback / Explanation: If a loan's LTV ratio is too high, a lender may require that the borrower purchase private mortgage insurance, commonly called PMI. This insurance secures whatever portion of the loan amount takes the LTV ratio above the typical ratio limit – which is usually 80% of the property's value. This protects the lender from the borrower's default – which can become more likely if the borrower has very little equity in the property.

Question: Which of the following is a pre-calculated, unchanging number that relates the principal and interest payment amount of a loan to the amount of the loan at a given interest rate and term?
Feedback / Explanation: With the loan constant in hand, one can easily derive the amount of a loan from its principal and interest payment and the amount of the principal and interest payment from the loan amount.

Question: Which of the following is the secondary loan market for American agricultural businesses?
Feedback / Explanation: The Federal Agricultural Mortgage Corporation, or Farmer Mac. Farmer Mac (FAMC) is a federal agency whose central function is to increase the availability and affordability of credit for American agriculture and American rural communities.

Question: Mortgage financing is the practice of
A: buying and selling mortgages as an investment vehicle
B: lending money to real estate investors to finance the purchase of mortgages
C: using borrowed funds secured by a mortgage or trust deed to purchase real estate
D: obtaining equitable title to real estate by buying promissory notes
Feedback / Explanation: When a borrower gives a note promising to repay the borrowed money and executes a mortgage on the real estate for which the money is being borrowed as security, the financing method is called mortgage financing. The term "mortgage financing" also applies to real estate loans secured by a deed of trust

Question: What is a lien-theory state?
A: A state in which a lienor holds legal title to a secured property
B: A state in which a mortgage is considered to be a lien against a secured property
C: A state that allows a real estate owner's creditors to record liens against the owner's property
D: A state in which a lien is considered as a conveyance
Feedback / Explanation: States differ in their interpretation of who owns mortgaged property. Those that regard the mortgage as a lien held by the mortgagee (lender) against the property owned by the mortgagor (borrower) are called lien-theory states. Those that regard the mortgage document as a conveyance of ownership from the mortgagor to the mortgagee are called title-theory states. Some states interpret ownership of mortgaged property from a point of view that combines aspects of both title and lien theory

Question: What is the function of a note in a mortgage or trust deed financing arrangement?
A: It is evidence of the lender's interest in the collateral property
B: It is evidence of ownership of the mortgage or trust deed
C: It contains the borrower's promise to maintain the value of the property given as collateral for a loan
D: It is evidence of the borrower's debt to the lender
Feedback / Explanation: A valid mortgage or trust deed financing arrangement requires a note as evidence of the debt

Question: The document that provides evidence that a certain property is pledged as collateral for a loan is the
Feedback / Explanation: A mortgage is a legal document stating the pledge of the borrower (the mortgagor) to the lender (the mortgagee). The mortgage document pledges the borrower's ownership interest in the real estate in question as collateral against performance of the debt obligation

Question: The borrower in a mortgage loan transaction is known as the
Feedback / Explanation: The mortgagor is the borrower and the mortgagee is the lender. As a memory aid, notice that "lender" and "mortgagee" both have two "e"s. "Mortgagor" and "borrower" both have two "o"s

Question: If a borrower obtains an interest-only loan of $750,000 at an annual interest rate of 8%, what is the monthly interest payment?
If a borrower's monthly interest payment on an interest-only loan at an annual interest rate of 9% is $3,750, how much was the loan amount?
A borrower of a $950,000 interest-only loan makes annual interest payments of $83,125.  What interest rate is the borrower paying?
How much is a discount point?
D: It depends on the interest rate and the loan amount
Feedback / Explanation: A discount point is one percent of the loan amount. Thus, one point on a $100,000 loan equals $1,000

Question: Which of the following is true of an amortizing loan?
A: The amount of annual interest paid is the same for every year of the loan term
B: Part of each periodic payment is applied to repayment of the loan balance in advance and part is applied to payment of interest in arrears
C: Except for any points that may be paid, the interest on the loan balance is usually paid in advance
D: The interest rate is reduced each year to maintain equal payments even though the outstanding loan balance is smaller
Feedback / Explanation: In an amortizing loan, part of the principal is repaid periodically along with interest, so that the principal balance decreases over the life of the loan. The annual interest is never the same, since the principal balance to which the interest rate applies changes every year. Interest on a loan is always paid in arrears, not in advance

Question: For a loan that is not backed by the Federal Housing Administration or Veterans Administration, and for which the borrower is making a down payment of less than 20%, the lender is likely require the borrower to obtain
Feedback / Explanation: The lender may require the borrower to obtain private mortgage insurance, or PMI. Mortgage insurance protects the lender against loss of a portion of the loan (typically 20-25%) in case of borrower default. Private mortgage insurance generally applies to loans that are not backed by the Federal Housing Administration (FHA) or Veterans Administration (VA) and that have a down payment of less than 20% of the property value

Question: What is a loan-to-value ratio?
A: The percentage of a lender's portfolio that is composed of mortgage loans
B: The ratio of borrowed principal plus total interest to the appraised value of the collateral property
C: The ratio of a lender's return on a mortgage loan to the value of the collateral property
D: The fraction of the appraised value of the property offered as collateral which the lender is willing to lend
Feedback / Explanation: The relationship of the loan amount to the property value, expressed as a percentage, is called the loan-to-value ratio, or LTV. If the lender's loan to value ratio is 80%, the lender will lend only $80,000 on a home appraised at $100,000. The difference between what the lender will lend and what the borrower must pay for the property is the amount the borrower must provide in cash as a down payment

Question: The difference between what a borrower has to pay to purchase a property and the amount a lender will lend on the property is the
Feedback / Explanation: Price less loan is the down payment. This is also the buyer's initial equity

Question: The Equal Credit Opportunity Act prohibits a lender from
A: refusing a loan because the property is located in a certain area
B: including income from self-employment in the borrower's qualifying income
C: requiring both spouses to sign the loan application form
D: refusing a loan because a borrower has a defective credit report
Feedback / Explanation: The Equal Credit Opportunity Act (ECOA) requires a lender to evaluate a loan applicant on the basis of that applicant's own income and credit rating, unless the applicant requests the inclusion of another's income and credit rating in the application. In addition, ECOA has prohibited a number of practices in mortgage loan underwriting, including refusing a loan based on an applicant’s demographic characteristics

Question: A loan applicant has an annual gross income of $36,000. How much will a lender allow the applicant to pay for monthly housing expense to qualify for a loan if the lender uses an income ratio of 28%?
If a lender discovers that an applicant for a mortgage loan has borrowed the down payment from a relative and has to repay that loan, the lender is likely to
B: adjust the applicant's debt ratio calculation and lower the loan amount
C: increase the loan amount to enable the borrower to pay off the loan to the relative
D: require the borrower to make payments to an escrow account for repayment of the relative's loan
Feedback / Explanation: If someone such as a relative is lending an applicant a portion of the down payment with a provision for repayment, a lender will consider this another debt obligation and adjust the debt ratio accordingly. This can lower the amount a lender is willing to lend

Question: The Federal Reserve's Regulation Z applies to which loans?
B: All loans secured by real estate
C: All loans secured by a residence
Feedback / Explanation: Regulation Z applies to all loans secured by a residence. It does not apply to commercial loans or to agricultural loans over $25,000. Its provisions cover the disclosure of costs, the right to rescind the credit transaction, advertising credit offers, and penalties for non-compliance with the act

Question: If a particular loan falls under Regulation Z's right of rescission provision,
A: the lender has the right to change the terms of the loan within a certain period
B: the lender has the right to accelerate repayment of the loan because of a change in the borrower's credit status
C: the borrower has the right to pay off the loan ahead of schedule with no penalty
D: the borrower has a limited right to cancel the transaction within a certain period
Feedback / Explanation: A borrower has a limited right to cancel the credit transaction, usually within three days of completion of the transaction. The right of rescission does not apply to "residential mortgage transactions," that is, to mortgage loans used to finance the purchase or construction of the borrower's primary residence. It does, however, apply to refinancing of mortgage loans, and to home equity loans. State law may require a rescission period and notice on first mortgage loan transactions as well

Question: Under the Equal Credit Opportunity Act, a lender, or a real estate agent who assists a seller in qualifying a potential buyer, may not
A: tell a rejected loan applicant the reasons for the rejection
B: ask the buyer/borrower about his/her religion or national origin
C: ask the buyer/borrower to explain gaps in his/her employment history
D: use a credit report if the loan applicant disputes any information in the report
Feedback / Explanation: ECOA prohibits discrimination in extending credit based on race, color, religion, national origin, sex, marital status, age, or dependency upon public assistance. A creditor may not make any statements to discourage an applicant on the basis of such discrimination or ask any questions of an applicant concerning these discriminatory items. A real estate licensee who assists a seller in qualifying a potential buyer may fall within the reach of this prohibition

Question: A conventional mortgage loan is one that is
A: backed by the Federal National Mortgage Association
B: insured under Section 203(b) of the Federal Housing Administration loan program
C: guaranteed by the Government National Mortgage Association
Feedback / Explanation: A conventional mortgage loan is a permanent long-term loan that is not FHA-insured or VA-guaranteed. FNMA does not "back" loans; FHA only insures FHA loans; and the VA, not GNMA guarantees loans

Question: The assumability of an FHA-insured loan is
B: limited by when the loan was originated and by the type of property
D: prohibited on all existing loans under current regulations
Feedback / Explanation: Rules for assumability vary according to when the FHA-insured loan was originated and whether the original loan was for an investment property or an owner-occupied principal residence

Question: A VA certificate of eligibility determines
A: whether an individual is a veteran
B: the maximum loan amount an approved lender can give to veterans
C: how much of a loan the VA will guarantee
D: whether a lender is approved to issue VA-guaranteed loans
Feedback / Explanation: A veteran must apply for a Certificate of Eligibility to find out how much the VA will guarantee in a particular situation

Question: A borrower obtains a 30-year, fully amortizing mortgage loan of $300,000 at 8%.  What is the principal balance at the end of the loan term?
Feedback / Explanation: In a fully amortizing mortgage, the principal balance is zero at the end of the term

Question: Which of the following describes a purchase money mortgage financing arrangement?
A: A bank gives a buyer a senior mortgage loan that fully covers the cost of purchasing the property
B: The buyer gives the seller a mortgage and note as part of the purchase price of the property
C: A land trust holds title to the property while the buyer makes periodic installment payments to the seller
D: The seller uses the purchase money obtained from the buyer's mortgage loan to repay the seller's outstanding loan balance
Feedback / Explanation: With a purchase money mortgage, the borrower gives a mortgage and note to the seller to finance some or all of the purchase price of the property. The seller in this case is said to "take back" a note, or to "carry paper," on the property

Question: Barry is borrowing 300,000 at 5% for 30 years. The loan constant for this loan is 5.37. What will Barry have to pay for principal and interest each month?
Feedback / Explanation: $300,000/1,000 is 300 units times the loan constant of 5.37 = $1,611

Question: Barry, from the previous question, is buying a house that appraises for $375,000, but the lender will lend him no more than $300,000, in spite of Barry’s excellent income, debt and credit qualifications. This is most likely because
A: the lender’s loan-to-value (LTV) ratio is fixed at 80%.
B: the loan constant determines the maximum loan.
C: the lender limits all loans to $300,000.
D: the lender believes the appraised value is inflated.
Feedback / Explanation: To protect further against loss, a lender will usually lend only a portion of the property's value. The relationship of the loan amount to the property value, expressed as a percentage, is called the loan-to-value ratio, or LTV. If the lender's loan to value ratio is 80%, the lender will lend only $80,000 on a home appraised at $100,000. The difference between what the lender will lend and what the borrower must pay for the property is the amount the borrower must provide in cash as a down payment.

Question: All investors desire their investments to increase in value. However,
A: the degree of return is inversely related to the degree of risk
B: the more the investor stands to gain, the greater the risk that the investor may lose
C: investments requiring intense management have lesser returns
D: the more liquid an investment is, the greater the chances are that the investment will not appreciate
Feedback / Explanation: The general rule in investments is that the safer the investment, the more slowly it gains in value. The more you want it to gain, and the more quickly, the more you must risk losing it. Reward in investing corresponds directly to the degree of risk

Question: Two of the rewards that investments offer are
Feedback / Explanation: The basic financial rewards of an investment include income, appreciation in asset value, positive borrowing leverage, and tax benefits

Question: An investor invests in fifteen diversified bond funds. This is an example of an investment in
Feedback / Explanation: A debt investment is one in which an investor buys a debt instrument. Examples are bonds, notes, mortgages, and bond mutual funds. The basic reward comes in the form of interest. Debt investments are usually riskier than money investments and less risky than stocks or real estate

Question: A real estate investment can take a long period of time to sell. For the investor, this means that real estate is
Feedback / Explanation: Real estate is by nature more difficult to convert to cash than many other types of investment. Compare the selling of a home with the ease of drawing money out of a bank account or selling a stock

Question: Compared to a stock portfolio, a real estate investment would be considered
Feedback / Explanation: Real estate tends to require a high degree of investor involvement in management of the investment. Even raw land requires some degree of maintenance to preserve its value. Improved properties often require extensive management, including repairs, maintenance, onsite leasing, tenant relations, security, and fiscal management

Question: Six investors purchase a shopping center. One investor manages the tenants and another handles the marketing and leasing. Two investors manage accounting and finance, and the remaining two run the management office. This is a possible example of
Feedback / Explanation: A general partnership is a syndicate in which all members participate equally in managing the investment and in the profits or losses it generates

Question: Taxable income produced by an income property is
A: gross income minus expenses plus land and building depreciation
B: gross income minus expenses minus land and building depreciation
C: gross income minus building depreciation plus land depreciation
D: gross income minus expenses minus building depreciation
Feedback / Explanation: Taxable income from investment real estate is the gross income received minus any expenses, deductions or exclusions that current tax law allows. Depreciation of land is not allowed

Question: As a general rule, in deriving taxable income on an investment property, it is legal to
A: deduct principal and interest payments from income
D: deduct principal and interest payments from income and capital gain
Feedback / Explanation: Taxable income is net operating income minus all allowable deductions. The interest portion of debt service is allowed and is deducted from Net Operating Income as interest expense. Principal repayment is not an allowable deduction on investment properties. Capital gain derivation does not involve interest or principal payments

Question: Which of the following is true of the tax treatment of a principal residence?
A: The owner may deduct the property's interest and principal from ordinary income
B: The owner may depreciate the property and deduct depreciation expenses
C: The owner can deduct any capital gain when the property is sold
D: The owner may be able to exclude capital gain from taxable income when the property is sold
Feedback / Explanation: When real estate, whether non-income or income, is sold, a taxable event occurs. If the sale proceeds exceed the original cost of the investment, subject to some adjustments, there is a capital gain that is subject to tax. Tax law provides an exclusion of $250,000 for an individual taxpayer and $500,000 for married taxpayers filing jointly. The exclusion of gain from sale of a residence can be claimed every two years, provided the taxpayer:  1. owned the property for at least two years during the five years preceding the date of sale;  2. used the property as principal residence for a total of two years during that five-year period;  3. has waited two years since the last use of the exclusion for any sale

Question: An investment property seller pays $14,000 in closing costs. These costs
A: may be deducted from personal income
B: may be deducted from the property's income
C: may be deducted from the sale price for gains tax purposes
D: may be deducted from the adjusted basis for gains tax purposes
Feedback / Explanation: Selling costs include such expenses as brokerage commissions, relevant advertising, legal fees, seller-paid points and other closing costs. These costs are deducted from the selling price to get the amount realized, which is reduced by the adjusted basis to calculate gain on sale for gains tax purposes

Question: Capital gain tax is figured by multiplying one's tax bracket times
A: the sum of the beginning basis plus gain
B: the difference between net sale proceeds and adjusted basis
C: the sum of net sale proceeds and capital gain
D: the difference between net sale proceeds and capital gain
Feedback / Explanation: The IRS defines gain on the sale of a home as amount realized from the sale minus the adjusted basis of the home sold. The amount realized, also known as net proceeds from sale, is expressed by the formula: sale price - cost of sale = amount realized

Question: Cash flow is a measure of how much pre-tax or after-tax cash an investment property generates. To derive cash flow it is therefore necessary to exclude
Feedback / Explanation: Cash flow is the difference between the amount of actual cash flowing into the investment as revenue and out of the investment for expenses, debt service, and all other items. Cash flow concerns cash items only, and therefore excludes depreciation (cost recovery), which is not a cash expense

Question: One way investors measure the yield of an investment is by
A: dividing net operating income by cash flow
B: multiplying the investor's required yield times after-tax cash flow
C: dividing cash flow by the investor's equity
D: multiplying cash flow times the price paid for the property
Feedback / Explanation: cash flow ÷ equity = return on equity (ROE)

Question: Conservation of capital refers to
A: preserving, through low-risk investments, the original amount invested
B: increasing the value of the original investment over an amount of time
C: investing only in low cost and safe investment entities
D: getting conservative advice from your financial planner
Feedback / Explanation: With any investment, one wants the original investment to grow, without losing it. This idea is called conservation of capital

Question: Which of the following statements describes the general rule of investing?
A: The safer the investment, the slower the gain
B: The larger the company, the safer the investment
C: The less the cost per share, the safer the investment
D: The riskier the investment, the greater the gain
Feedback / Explanation: The general rule in investments is that the safer the investment, the more slowly it gains in value. The more you want it to gain, and the more quickly, the more you must risk losing it

Question: Which of the following terms is an example of an investment that is considered to be “liquid”?
Feedback / Explanation: The most liquid form of financial investment is generally cash, since cash is itself a medium of exchange and people always want it

Question: Which of the following may add value to one’s financial investment over a period of time?
Feedback / Explanation: Your invested asset itself may gain value over time because of an increase in market demand for it

Question: Investment risks include
B: changes in the value of money as an exchange medium
C: changes in the office staff of the company you have invested in
Feedback / Explanation: Changes in the value of money as an exchange medium, such as through inflation, may decrease the practical value of your invested resource

Question: Which of the following occurs when an investor is required to add to the investment just to keep it in place?
Feedback / Explanation: An invested asset may fail to appreciate as expected. It may even decline in value. Perhaps even worse, the investor may be called on to add to the investment just to maintain a required level of equity in the investment.   The loss of value of a an asset that is partially financed is called negative leverage

Question: Investing in an income-producing property would NOT include which of the following?
B: A retail store in a strip mall
D: An office building in a downtown location
Feedback / Explanation: Non-income property is a residential property used as the investor’s primary residence. Income property owned specifically for the investment rewards include multi-family residential properties, retail stores, industrial properties, and office buildings

Question: Investing in real estate is always subject to which of the following risk factor?
Feedback / Explanation: Capital put into real estate is always subject to the full range of risk factors: market changes, income shortfalls, negative leverage, tax law changes, and poor overall return

Question: Which of the following investment entities can comprise a real estate syndicate?
Feedback / Explanation: A real estate syndicate is a group of investors who combine resources to buy, develop, and/or operate a property.  A general partnership is a syndicate in which all members participate equally in managing the investment and in the profits or losses it generates

Question: Taxable income from investment real estate is
A: total income less expenses and cost of vacancies
C: gross income minus expenses, deductions and exclusions per tax law
Feedback / Explanation: Taxable income from investment real estate is the gross income received minus any expenses, deductions or exclusions that current tax law allows. Depreciation is also considered a deductible expense.  Taxable income from real estate is added to the investor's other income and taxed at the investor's marginal tax rate

Question: Cost recovery, or depreciation, allows the owner of income property to deduct a portion of the value of the property’s improvements from gross income over the life of the asset.  The depreciable life of the asset is defined by
B: the age of the property at purchase
C: the year built plus the number of years to date
D: the age of the property at sale
Feedback / Explanation: Cost recovery, or depreciation, allows the owner of income property to deduct a portion of the property's value from gross income each year over the life of the asset. The "life of the asset" and the deductible portion are defined by law

Question: Section 1031 (Starker) Exchanges are a way to defer taxes on the sale of investment property.  Tax on any gain is deferred until
A: the property is sold and not exchanged
B: any mortgage on the property is paid in full
C: owners qualify for regular tax exemptions
D: ten years after the 1031 Exchange closing date
Feedback / Explanation: An investor can sometimes defer the reporting of gain or loss, and, hence, taxation of gain, by participating in an exchange of like-kind assets. The legislation that deals with like-kind exchanges is contained in Section 1031 of the IRS code. Tax on gain is deferred until the investment or business property is sold and not exchanged

Question: Appreciation is the increase in the value of an asset over time.  For residential property, a simple formula to estimate total appreciation is
A: current value plus or minus the price at the time the property is ultimately sold
B: current value minus original purchase price
C: original purchase price plus cost of improvements
D: total cost of improvements over time of ownership minus current value
Feedback / Explanation: Appreciation is the increase in value of an asset over time. A simple way to estimate appreciation on a primary residence is to subtract the price originally paid from the estimated current market value

Question: Taxable income is
A: current market value minus depreciation and expenses for the current tax year
B: original purchase price minus any loss on the market value for the current tax year
C: net operating income minus all allowable deductions
Feedback / Explanation: The owner's tax liability on taxable income from the property is based on taxable income rather than cash flow, or income received minus any expense deductions allowed by current tax law (including depreciation)

Question: What is the total appreciation of a property that was purchased ten years ago for $300,000 and has a current market value of $325,000?
Feedback / Explanation: A simple way to estimate appreciation on a primary residence is to subtract the price originally paid from the estimated current market value: Current value - original price = total appreciation

Question: The interest on a home equity loan is deductible only if the loan proceeds were used to
A: pay off credit card debt and/or any outstanding debt on which interest was due
B: buy, build or make big improvements to the home that secures the loan
C: purchase a second or vacation home
D: assist family members with the purchase of a home
Feedback / Explanation: The interest on a home equity loan may be deducted only if the loan is used to “buy, build or substantially improve” the home that secures the loan. Principal payments on a loan are not deductible

Question: Which of the following is the primary tax benefit available to the owner of a non-income property?
A: The annual deduction for the full mortgage payment
B: The portion of annual mortgage payment that goes to repay the principal
C: The depreciation amounts available for non-income properties
D: The annual deduction for mortgage interest
Feedback / Explanation: The primary tax benefit available to the owner of a non-income property is the annual deduction for mortgage interest, assuming the owner itemizes deductions. The portion of mortgage payments applied to principal must be subtracted to determine the amount paid for interest. Principal repayment is not deductible

Question: If married taxpayers filing jointly qualified for the gains tax exclusion, what would be the amount of capital gains due on their principal residence if the original purchase price was $300,000 and the recent sale price was $425,000?
Feedback / Explanation: Tax law provides an exclusion of $250,000 for an individual taxpayer and $500,000 for married taxpayers filing jointly

Question: Which of the following expenses would NOT be considered in calculating the adjusted basis of a property for tax purposes?
Feedback / Explanation: The beginning basis is increased or decreased by certain types of expenditures made while the property is owned. Basis is increased by the cost of capital improvements made to the property

Question: Tax law provides for exclusions for individual and married taxpayers for capital gains purposes.  Those exclusion amounts are
Feedback / Explanation: Tax law provides an exclusion of $250,000 for an individual taxpayer and $500,000 for married taxpayers filing jointly

Question: Which of the following is required for a capital gains tax exclusion for individuals and married taxpayers?
A: The taxpayer must own the property for at least one of the last three years prior to the date of sale
B: The taxpayer must use the property as a principal residence for at least two years during the five years preceding the date of sale
C: The taxpayer must not occupy the property
D: The taxpayer must own and occupy the property for a period of 12 months and sell it at a loss  from the original purchase price
Feedback / Explanation: The exclusion of gain from sale of a residence can be claimed every two years, provided the taxpayer: 1. owned the property for at least two years during the five years preceding the date of sale; 2. used the property as principal residence for a total of two years during that five-year period; 3. has waited two years since the last use of the exclusion for any sale

Question: Cash flow is defined as
A: income minus expenses and debt service
B: the total sum of all income flowing into the investment
C: the total monthly income from the investment offset by 2/3 of the monthly expenses
D: the total monthly income from the investment offset by ½ of the monthly expenses
Feedback / Explanation: Investors are generally concerned with cash flow which is defined as the difference between the amount of actual cash flowing into the investment as revenue and out of the investment for expenses, debt service and all other items

Question: Taxable income is net operating income minus all allowable deductions.  Those allowable deductions include all of the following with the exception of
Feedback / Explanation: Taxable income is net operating income minus all allowable deductions. Cost recovery expense is allowed as a deduction, while allowances for reserves and payments on loan principal payback are not allowed

Question: Net operating income (NOI) is defined as
A: the income remaining after payment of all expenses
B: gross income minus expenses before debt service
C: gross income minus all debt service and depreciation
D: income from rents and tenant portions of payment for utilities and taxes
Feedback / Explanation: NOI is defined as the gross income minus expenses before debt service

Question: A real estate syndicate is defined as
A: a group of two or more people whose purpose is to invest in real estate to generate income
B: a closed group of people who own real estate
C: a system whereby real estate investors can exchange information
D: similar to an MLS, a compilation of listings that are available for investment
Feedback / Explanation: A real estate syndicate is a group of investors who combine resources to buy, develop, and/or operate a property

Question: A Real Estate Mortgage Investment Conduit (REMIC) is
A: a group of mortgage brokers whose goal is to reach more potential clients
B: an office within any of the large banking institutions that focuses only on private mortgage investors
C: a form of partnership created to hold a pool of mortgages secured by real property
D: a group of potential purchasers of real estate who individually do not qualify for a mortgage
Feedback / Explanation: A REMIC is a kind of partnership entity formed to hold a fixed pool of mortgages that are secured by real property. The entity issues two kinds of interest. Holders of residual interests are treated, for tax purposes, as partners. Holders of regular interests are regarded as owning debt instruments

Question: After-tax cash flow is defined as
A: pre-tax cash flow minus tax liability
B: pre-tax cash flow minus debt service minus depreciation
Feedback / Explanation: After-tax cash flow is cash flow (NOI – debt service) minus tax liability, which is derived by accounting for depreciation, reserves, interest, and principal payments

Question: Passive investment activities are defined as
A: those in which the investor does not actively participate
B: investment activity that requires the investor to take a role in the management of the company
C: investments that require no monitoring, but report activity on an annual basis
D: those which have no active investors
Feedback / Explanation: Passive activities are business activities in which the taxpayer does not materially participate, including interests in limited partnerships and rental activities; losses from such activities can be used to offset income from other passive activities

Question: REO, or “real estate owned” is a term used
A: primarily by lenders to indicate that the property is owned by the bank through foreclosure
B: to indicate which properties are owned v. rented
C: to indicate that a Realtor ® owns a property
D: as a designation that indicates “Real Estate Owner”.
Feedback / Explanation: REO – real estate owned is a term used primarily by lenders to indicate that the property is owned by the bank through default

Question: When contemplating an investment, all of the following are important risk factors to consider EXCEPT
B: changes in the value of money
Feedback / Explanation: Investment risks come from a variety of general sources, including the market, business operations, the value of money, and changes in the interest rate

Question: When an investment property generates less income than the amount the investor has to pay a lender to finance the investment, the investor suffers from
Feedback / Explanation: Leveraging is borrowing funds in order to make an investment that is larger than your own resource permits you to do directly. In negative leverage situations, your cost of borrowing funds to make the investment becomes greater than the income the investment returns to you

Question: An individual buys a small office building as an investment and participates actively in the management and operation of the building. This is an example of
Feedback / Explanation: Individuals, corporations or other investor entities may invest as active investors in a property by buying it directly and taking responsibility for managing and operating the property. This is called a direct investment

Question: Which of the following best describes a Real Estate Investment Trust?
A: Investors are partners in an entity that sells pools of mortgages secured by real property
B: Investors are general partners who pool cash resources to buy, develop and operate a property
C: Owners of an income property convey title in the property to a land trust as a tax shield
D: Investors own shares in a trust that receives 75% of its income from real estate investments
Feedback / Explanation: A trust must receive at least 75% of its income from real estate to qualify as a REIT

Question: Cost recovery is allowed as a federal tax deduction on
Feedback / Explanation: Cost recovery, or depreciation, allows the owner of income property to deduct a portion of the property's value from gross income each year over the life of the asset. Principal residences cannot be depreciated (unless portions are treated as a home office.) Further, land cannot be depreciated

Question: A homeowner paid $285,000 for a house three years ago.  The house sells today for $339,000. How much has the property appreciated?
Feedback / Explanation: Appreciation as a per cent can be estimated by (1) subtracting the estimated current market value from the price originally paid (339,000 - 285,000 = 54,000) and (2) dividing the result by the original price (54,000 / 285,000 = . 19 or 19%)

Question: The primary tax benefit in owning a non-income property such as a residence is
C: a deduction for costs of operating the property
Feedback / Explanation: Deducting mortgage interest is a significant tax advantage of owning one's residence. Depreciation and cost-deductions do not apply to a non-income producing residence. Appreciation is a benefit, but not a tax benefit

Question: A house sold for $350,000.  The seller paid a brokerage commission of six percent, legal fees of $600, and had other closing costs of $3,000.  What are the net proceeds from the sale?
Feedback / Explanation: Amount realized. The amount realized, also known as net proceeds from sale, is expressed by the formula:  sale price - cost of sale = amount realize. Sale price: $350,000. Cost of sale: 6% (350,000 x 0.06 = $21,000) + $600 + $3,000 = $24,600. Net proceeds of sale: $325,400

Question: A homeowner bought a house five years ago for $250,000.  Since then, the homeowner has spent $6,000 to pave the driveway and has added a central heating/air-conditioning system at a cost of $10,000.  What is the homeowner's adjusted basis if the house is sold today?
Feedback / Explanation: The beginning basis is the cost of acquiring a property. Basis is increased by capital improvements and decreased by depreciation. The basic formula for adjusted basis is Beginning basis ($250,000) + capital improvements ($6,000 + $10,000) - depreciation ($0) = adjusted basis ($266,000)

Question: A homeowner sold her house and had net proceeds of $191,000.  Her adjusted basis in the home was $176,000. She immediately bought another house for $200,000.  What was her capital gain?
Feedback / Explanation: Gain on sale. The gain on sale of a primary residence is represented by the basic formula:  amount realized (net sales proceeds) - adjusted basis = gain on sale.  $191,000 - $176,000 = $15,000

Question: After three years of owner-occupancy, a young homeowner sells his principal residence for a gain of $150,000, and the next month buys another principal residence that costs more than the adjusted sale price of the old home.  Which of the following is true of the treatment of the tax on gain?
B: It must be paid in the year of the sale
C: The homeowner may choose to pay it or defer it
D: Tax is due on the difference between the cost of the new home and adjusted basis of the old one
Feedback / Explanation: There is no taxable gain because the gain falls under the exclusion limit of $250,000. Tax law provides an exclusion of $250,000 for an individual taxpayer and $500,000 for married taxpayers filing jointly on the gain from sale of a principal residence. The seller must have owned the property for at least two years during the five years preceding the date of sale; used the property as principal residence for a total of two years during that five-year period; and have waited two years since the last use of the exclusion for any sale

Question: Which of the following are limitations on the exclusion of capital gain on the sale of a house?
A: The seller is 55 years old, the property is used as a principal residence, and the seller's previously claimed exclusions do not exceed $125,000
B: The property is owner-occupied and the seller has never claimed an exclusion previously
C: The seller must have owned it for two years of the previous five, used it as a principal residence for two years during that period, and not claimed an exclusion in the previous two years
D: The seller must have owned and occupied it for five years and not claimed an exclusion in the previous year
Feedback / Explanation: Tax law provides an exclusion of $250,000 for an individual taxpayer and $500,000 for married taxpayers filing jointly. Exclusion of gain from sale of a residence can be claimed every two years, provided the taxpayer:  1. owned the property for at least two years during the five years preceding the date of sale; 2. used the property as principal residence for a total of two years during that five-year period; 3. has waited two years since the last use of the exclusion for any sale

Question: Bill Holdfast owns a small retail property that he inherited from his father.  There are no mortgages or interest expenses connected with the property.  Bill takes an annual cost recovery expense of $5,000. The property has a monthly gross income of $1,500 and monthly operating expenses of $500. Bill's taxable income from this property will be taxed at a rate of 24%.  What is the tax liability for the year?
Feedback / Explanation: Annual gross operating income ($1,500 x 12 = $18,000) - annual operating expenses ($500 x 12 = $6,000) = annual net operating income ( $12,000); annual net operating income ($12,000) - cost recovery expense ($5,000) = taxable income ($7,000); taxable income ($7,000) x tax rate (24%) = tax liability ($1,680)
